content.body: Spectroscopy identifies molecular structure using different types of radiation. The three main techniques are infrared (IR), mass spectrometry (MS), and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR).

IR Spectroscopy: identifies functional groups
- O-H stretch: 2500-3300 cm⁻¹ (carboxylic acid, broad)
- O-H stretch: 3200-3600 cm⁻¹ (alcohol)
- C=O stretch: 1630-1820 cm⁻¹
- N-H: 3300-3500 cm⁻¹

Mass Spec: M⁺ peak = molecular mass
Fragmentation pattern identifies structure

content.title: Reading an IR Spectrum
content.body:
If an IR spectrum shows:
- Broad absorption at 2500-3300 cm⁻¹ → carboxylic acid O-H
- Sharp absorption at ~1720 cm⁻¹ → C=O
Conclusion: compound contains COOH group (carboxylic acid)

questions.question: What does the M⁺ peak in mass spectrometry tell you?
questions.explanation: The molecular ion peak (M⁺) corresponds to the molecular mass of the compound

questions.question: A broad IR absorption at 2500-3300 cm⁻¹ suggests:
questions.explanation: Carboxylic acids show a characteristic broad O-H stretch in this region

questions.question: C=O stretching frequency in IR appears around:
questions.explanation: The C=O bond absorbs at approximately 1630-1820 cm⁻¹

questions.question: NMR spectroscopy is based on:
options: Proton (hydrogen nucleus) spin in a magnetic field
questions.answer: Proton (hydrogen nucleus) spin in a magnetic field
questions.explanation: ¹H NMR detects hydrogen atoms in different chemical environments

questions.question: In mass spectrometry, the base peak is:
questions.explanation: The base peak is the tallest peak in the mass spectrum — the most common fragment

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

content.body: Spectroscopic techniques allow chemists to determine the structure of organic compounds. The main techniques at A-level are mass spectrometry (MS), infrared spectroscopy (IR), and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR).

content.title: Mass Spectrometry
content.body:
Principle: molecules are vaporised and ionised. Ions accelerated and deflected by magnetic field. Detected by mass/charge ratio (m/z).

Molecular ion peak (M⁺): gives molecular mass
Base peak: most abundant fragment
Fragmentation pattern helps identify structure

Isotope peaks: M+1 for ¹³C, M+2 for Cl and Br
Chlorine: M and M+2 peaks in 3:1 ratio (³⁵Cl:³⁷Cl)
Bromine: M and M+2 peaks in 1:1 ratio (⁷⁹Br:⁸¹Br)

content.title: NMR Spectroscopy
content.body:
¹H NMR detects proton environments in a molecule
Chemical shift (δ, ppm) indicates type of proton:
• TMS (reference): δ = 0
• CH₃−: δ ≈ 1
• −CH₂−: δ ≈ 1.5
• −OH: δ ≈ 2-5
• −CHO: δ ≈ 9-10
• ArH (aromatic): δ ≈ 7-8

Splitting pattern: n+1 rule (n adjacent H's → n+1 peaks)

questions.question: The molecular ion peak (M⁺) in mass spectrometry gives...
questions.explanation: The molecular ion peak M⁺ is formed when the molecule loses one electron. Its m/z value = relative molecular mass.

questions.question: In mass spectrometry, if two peaks appear at M and M+2 in 3:1 ratio, the compound contains...
questions.explanation: ³⁵Cl:³⁷Cl = 3:1 abundance ratio, giving M and M+2 peaks in 3:1 ratio.

questions.question: TMS is used in NMR as...
questions.explanation: Tetramethylsilane (TMS) is a universal reference with all equivalent protons, set at δ = 0 ppm.

questions.question: An aldehyde proton (−CHO) appears at δ ≈ ...
questions.explanation: Aldehyde protons are very deshielded (due to adjacent C=O) and appear at δ = 9-10 ppm.

questions.question: In NMR, the n+1 rule predicts the number of...
questions.explanation: A proton with n equivalent adjacent protons is split into n+1 lines (doublet, triplet, quartet etc.).

questions.question: Bromine gives M and M+2 peaks in ratio...
questions.explanation: ⁷⁹Br and ⁸¹Br are nearly equal in abundance (about 50:50), giving M and M+2 peaks of equal height.

questions.question: Infrared spectroscopy identifies...
questions.explanation: IR spectroscopy detects bond vibrations. Characteristic absorption frequencies identify functional groups (−OH, C=O, N−H etc.).

questions.question: A CH₃ group adjacent to no other H's gives how many NMR peaks?
questions.explanation: n+1 rule: CH₃ with 0 adjacent H's → 0+1 = 1 peak (singlet).

questions.question: In a mass spectrum, the base peak is...
questions.explanation: The base peak is the tallest peak in the mass spectrum — the most abundant ion formed in fragmentation.

questions.question: Aromatic protons (ArH) appear in NMR at δ ≈ ...
questions.explanation: Aromatic ring protons are deshielded by the ring current of delocalized electrons, appearing at δ = 7-8 ppm.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body:
Mass Spectrometry stages:
1. Ionisation (electron bombardment)
2. Acceleration (electric field)
3. Deflection (magnetic field) — lighter/more charged ions deflect more
4. Detection

m/z ratio: mass-to-charge ratio
Molecular ion peak (M⁺): gives molecular mass
Base peak: most abundant fragment
Isotope peaks: M+1, M+2 (e.g. ³⁵Cl/³⁷Cl)

Chromatography: separates mixtures based on different affinities for stationary vs mobile phase

content.title: Mass Spectrum Interpretation
content.body:
Ethanol (M=46): M⁺ peak at m/z=46
Fragments: m/z=45 (loss of H, CH₃CH₂O⁺), m/z=31 (CH₂OH⁺), m/z=29 (CHO⁺)

Chlorine isotopes: ³⁵Cl:³⁷Cl = 3:1
CH₃Cl M⁺: peaks at m/z=50 and m/z=52 in 3:1 ratio

content.title: Types of Chromatography
content.body:
TLC (thin layer): silica on plate, UV/iodine visualization, Rf = distance spot/distance solvent
Gas chromatography (GC): volatile compounds, separated by column, identified by retention time
HPLC: high-performance liquid, non-volatile compounds
Column chromatography: large-scale separation
GC-MS: combined — separate then identify each component by mass spectrum

questions.question: The molecular ion peak in a mass spectrum gives
options: the molecular mass of the compound
questions.answer: the molecular mass of the compound
questions.explanation: M⁺ peak: the intact molecule minus one electron. m/z value (for z=1) = molecular mass

questions.question: In mass spectrometry, ions are separated by
questions.explanation: Magnetic field deflects ions: lighter (smaller m) and more charged (larger z) deflect more → different paths

questions.question: Chlorine has two isotopes ³⁵Cl:³⁷Cl = 3:1. Compound CH₃Cl shows M⁺ peaks at m/z=50 and 52 in ratio
questions.explanation: ³⁵Cl compound: m/z=50. ³⁷Cl compound: m/z=52. Natural abundance ratio 3:1 reflects in spectrum

questions.question: Rf value in TLC =
questions.explanation: Rf = (distance travelled by spot)/(distance travelled by solvent front). Characteristic for each compound in given system

questions.question: Gas chromatography separates compounds based on
options: different boiling points and affinities for stationary phase
questions.answer: different boiling points and affinities for stationary phase
questions.explanation: GC: mobile phase is inert gas (He/N₂); compounds partition between gas and column stationary phase, separated by affinity and volatility

questions.question: GC-MS is powerful because
options: gas chromatography separates mixture, mass spectrometry identifies each component
questions.answer: gas chromatography separates mixture, mass spectrometry identifies each component
questions.explanation: GC separates complex mixtures; MS provides structural identification of each separated compound — powerful combined technique

questions.question: Base peak in mass spectrum is
options: most abundant fragment (highest peak, normalized to 100%)
questions.answer: most abundant fragment (highest peak, normalized to 100%)
questions.explanation: Base peak: most stable fragment, easiest bond to break. Spectra normalized with base peak = 100%

questions.question: Fragmentation in mass spectrum helps determine
options: structural information — which bonds break tells us groups present
questions.answer: structural information — which bonds break tells us groups present
questions.explanation: Fragmentation pattern: each peak corresponds to a fragment. Identifying fragments reveals molecular structure

questions.question: In column chromatography, compound with stronger affinity for stationary phase
questions.explanation: Stronger interaction with stationary phase → moves slower → elutes later. Weaker interaction → elutes earlier

questions.question: M+1 peak in mass spectrum is caused by
options: one ¹³C instead of ¹²C in the molecule (natural abundance ~1.1% per C)
questions.answer: one ¹³C instead of ¹²C in the molecule (natural abundance ~1.1% per C)
questions.explanation: ¹³C natural abundance is 1.1%. Compound with n carbons has M+1 peak of n×1.1% relative to M⁺. Used to count carbons

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body:
¹H NMR (Nuclear Magnetic Resonance):
Protons in different environments → different chemical shifts δ (ppm)
TMS reference (δ=0)

Chemical shift ranges:
δ 0-2: alkyl H (C-H)
δ 2-4: adjacent to C=O or halogen
δ 4-5: vinyl, C=CH, O-CH
δ 6-8: aromatic H
δ 9-10: aldehyde H
δ 10-12: carboxylic acid H

Spin-spin splitting (n+1 rule):
n adjacent H → n+1 peaks

content.title: NMR Interpretation
content.body:
Ethanol CH₃CH₂OH:
CH₃: δ~1.2, triplet (split by 2H of CH₂)
CH₂: δ~3.7, quartet (split by 3H of CH₃)
OH: δ~3.5, singlet (no adjacent H)

Integration ratios: CH₃:CH₂:OH = 3:2:1

Propan-2-one (CH₃COCH₃): one peak only (all 6H equivalent), δ~2.1

content.title: Combined Identification Strategy
content.body:
1. Molecular formula from mass spectrum (M⁺)
2. Degree of unsaturation = (2C+2+N-H)/2
3. IR: identify functional groups (C=O, O-H, N-H, C≡N)
4. ¹H NMR: number/type of H environments, integration, splitting
5. ¹³C NMR: number of C environments
6. Cross-reference all evidence → structural formula

questions.question: In ¹H NMR, TMS is used as reference because
options: it has one sharp peak at δ=0, away from most organic signals, is inert and volatile
questions.answer: it has one sharp peak at δ=0, away from most organic signals, is inert and volatile
questions.explanation: TMS (tetramethylsilane): 12 equivalent H, one sharp δ=0 peak, unreactive, easy to remove (bp 27°C)

questions.question: Aldehyde H appears at δ
questions.explanation: Aldehyde H (R-CHO) appears at δ 9-10 — highly deshielded by adjacent C=O

questions.question: n+1 rule: CH₃ next to CH₂ gives CH₃ signal as
questions.explanation: CH₃ has 2 adjacent H (from CH₂): n=2, n+1=3 peaks = triplet

questions.question: Integration in ¹H NMR gives
options: number of H in each environment (relative)
questions.answer: number of H in each environment (relative)
questions.explanation: Integration (area under peak) is proportional to number of equivalent protons

questions.question: Aromatic H appears at δ
questions.explanation: Aromatic (benzene ring) H: highly deshielded by ring current, δ 6.5-8.5

questions.question: Degree of unsaturation = (2C+2-H)/2 for CₙHₘ. For C₄H₈: DoU =
questions.explanation: DoU=(2×4+2-8)/2=(10-8)/2=1. One degree: one double bond or one ring

questions.question: ¹³C NMR of propan-2-one (CH₃COCH₃) shows how many peaks?
questions.explanation: Two types of C: CH₃ (both equivalent) and C=O. Only 2 peaks in ¹³C NMR

questions.question: IR absorption at ~1700 cm⁻¹ indicates
questions.explanation: C=O stretch: strong absorption ~1680-1750 cm⁻¹. Exact position distinguishes ketone, aldehyde, acid, ester

questions.question: Broad IR absorption at 2500-3300 cm⁻¹ with peak at ~1710 cm⁻¹ indicates
questions.explanation: Carboxylic acid: broad O-H stretch 2500-3300 cm⁻¹ (H-bonded) AND C=O stretch ~1710 cm⁻¹

questions.question: Strategy for identifying unknown organic compound: correct order is
options: mass spectrum (Mᵣ) → IR (functional groups) → NMR (H environments) → propose structure
questions.answer: mass spectrum (Mᵣ) → IR (functional groups) → NMR (H environments) → propose structure
questions.explanation: Systematic approach: establish molecular mass, identify functional groups, then H environments, cross-check with fragments

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body:
Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR):
Magnetic nuclei (¹H, ¹³C) in magnetic field absorb radiofrequency radiation
Chemical shift δ (ppm): position of absorption relative to TMS (δ=0)
Different environments → different δ values
Splitting (coupling): neighbouring H atoms split peaks
Integration: area ∝ number of H atoms

Common ¹H chemical shifts:
TMS: 0
CH₃ in alkane: ~1 ppm
C=C-H: ~5-6 ppm
Aromatic H: ~7-8 ppm
Aldehyde CHO: ~9-10 ppm
COOH: ~11-12 ppm

content.title: Splitting Pattern
content.body:
n adjacent H atoms: (n+1) peaks
0 neighbours: singlet
1 neighbour: doublet
2 neighbours: triplet
3 neighbours: quartet

Ethanol CH₃CH₂OH:
CH₃: quartet (3 adjacent H on CH₂) at ~1.2ppm
CH₂: quartet (split by CH₃, triplet) at ~3.7ppm
OH: singlet (D₂O exchanges) at ~2-5ppm

content.title: Interpreting Spectra
content.body:
Step 1: Count signals → number of H environments
Step 2: Integration → relative number of H
Step 3: Chemical shifts → chemical environment
Step 4: Splitting → number of adjacent H

Molecular formula + NMR → structure determination

questions.question: NMR spectroscopy is used to determine
options: arrangement of hydrogen atoms in molecule (structure)
questions.answer: arrangement of hydrogen atoms in molecule (structure)
questions.explanation: ¹H NMR: identifies number and type of H environments, connectivity → determine molecular structure

questions.question: Chemical shift δ is measured relative to
questions.explanation: TMS (Si(CH₃)₄): all H equivalent, set to δ=0. All other chemical shifts measured relative to this

questions.question: Aromatic H (benzene ring) appears at approximately
questions.explanation: Aromatic H: deshielded by ring current, δ≈7-8 ppm

questions.question: Aldehyde CHO proton appears far downfield (9-10 ppm) because
options: it is adjacent to double bond only
it is strongly deshielded by adjacent electronegative oxygen and C=O
questions.answer: it is strongly deshielded by adjacent electronegative oxygen and C=O
questions.explanation: Downfield = deshielded. Aldehyde H deshielded by C=O: electrons pulled away → bare proton resonates at low field

questions.question: n+1 rule: CH₃ group (3H) adjacent to another CH₂ (2H) gives
questions.explanation: Splitting by adjacent group: CH₃ split by 2H of adjacent CH₂: 2+1=3 peaks = triplet

questions.question: Integration in NMR tells us
options: relative number of H atoms in each signal
questions.answer: relative number of H atoms in each signal
questions.explanation: Integration: area under each peak is proportional to number of H atoms in that environment

questions.question: Equivalent hydrogen atoms give
questions.explanation: Chemically equivalent H atoms are in identical environments → same chemical shift → one signal

questions.question: D₂O addition to NMR sample causes OH and NH peaks to disappear because
options: exchangeable protons replaced by deuterium (not NMR active)
questions.answer: exchangeable protons replaced by deuterium (not NMR active)
questions.explanation: ¹H NMR detects ¹H. D₂O exchanges OH/NH₂ protons for ²H (NMR silent) → those peaks vanish → confirms -OH or -NH

questions.question: CH₃CH₂Br ¹H NMR: CH₂ signal is split into how many peaks?
questions.explanation: CH₂ split by adjacent CH₃ (3H): n+1 = 4 peaks (quartet)

questions.question: ¹³C NMR advantage over ¹H NMR
options: shows carbon skeleton (each distinct C environment gives one peak)
questions.answer: shows carbon skeleton (each distinct C environment gives one peak)
questions.explanation: ¹³C NMR: each type of carbon gives one peak → map the carbon framework of molecule directly

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body:
Mass Spectrometry:
1. Vaporise sample
2. Ionise (electron bombardment): M → M⁺ (molecular ion, m/z = M_r)
3. Accelerate ions
4. Deflect in magnetic field (heavier → less deflected)
5. Detect

m/z ratio (charge=1 usually): measures relative mass
Molecular ion peak (M⁺): gives molecular mass
Fragment ion peaks: structural information
Base peak: most abundant fragment

content.title: MS Fragmentation
content.body:
Butane (C₄H₁₀, M_r=58): M⁺ at m/z=58
Common fragments:
m/z=43: CH₃CH₂CH₂⁺ or CH₃CO⁺
m/z=29: C₂H₅⁺
m/z=15: CH₃⁺

Isotope peaks: M+1, M+2 peaks
35Cl:37Cl = 3:1 → two M⁺ peaks in ratio 3:1 for chloro compounds

content.title: Chromatography
content.body:
TLC (Thin Layer): Rf = distance moved by spot / distance moved by solvent
Column: separate mixture by differential adsorption
GLC (Gas-Liquid): volatile compounds separated by interaction with stationary phase
HPLC: high pressure liquid chromatography
Retention time in GLC: identifies compounds (compare to standards)

questions.question: Molecular ion peak in mass spectrum gives
questions.explanation: M⁺ peak: intact molecule ionised. m/z (z=1) = relative molecular mass

questions.question: Base peak in mass spectrum is
questions.explanation: Base peak: assigned 100% relative intensity — most stable/abundant fragment

questions.question: Chlorine has isotopes ³⁵Cl:³⁷Cl ≈ 3:1. A compound with one Cl shows molecular ion peaks ratio of
questions.explanation: One Cl: peaks at M (³⁵Cl) and M+2 (³⁷Cl) in ratio 3:1 — diagnostic for chloro compounds

questions.question: In mass spectrometer, ions are separated by
questions.explanation: Magnetic field deflects ions by m/z. Different m/z → different paths → separated → detected

questions.question: Rf value in TLC =
questions.explanation: Rf = distance travelled by spot / distance travelled by solvent. 0 < Rf < 1

questions.question: Higher Rf value means compound
options: less strongly adsorbed, moves more with solvent (less polar)
questions.answer: less strongly adsorbed, moves more with solvent (less polar)
questions.explanation: High Rf: compound moves far with solvent → interacts strongly with mobile phase (non-polar) → less polar compound

questions.question: GLC retention time is used to
options: identify compounds by comparing to known standards under same conditions
questions.answer: identify compounds by comparing to known standards under same conditions
questions.explanation: In GLC: each compound exits column after characteristic time (retention time) under given conditions — identification by comparison

questions.question: Mass spectrum fragment at m/z=15 typically indicates
questions.explanation: m/z=15: mass of CH₃ = 15 → methyl cation. Common fragment in compounds containing methyl groups

questions.question: HPLC advantage over simple column chromatography
options: much faster, higher resolution, quantitative due to high pressure mobile phase
questions.answer: much faster, higher resolution, quantitative due to high pressure mobile phase
questions.explanation: HPLC: high pressure pumps mobile phase through fine particle column → faster, better separation, quantitative

questions.question: In electron ionisation MS, M+1 peak is due to
questions.explanation: ¹³C at 1.1% abundance: some molecules have one ¹³C → mass one unit higher → M+1 peak. Intensity ∝ number of C atoms

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.
